quote: it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar as long as you have a credit score above 670. So would that qualify you?
stewart_read: Holland states the threshold as 670 — schema-compliant. This is worth noting because SESSION18_fcbf33c1 shows a rep stating 650. Holland gets this right. The qualifier delivery is clean and Nancy confirms without friction.
coaching_implication: Positive execution — use this clip when coaching the floor on credit-qualifier delivery. Holland's phrasing is the standard to hold others to.

quote: K. Well, yeah, I can definitely get you set up to learn more. And I can kinda tell you the info I know. I'm just a scheduler, so I don't know too much.
stewart_read: Holland's self-deprecation ('I'm just a scheduler, I don't know too much') is reactive hedging, not strategic positioning. The schema's universal escape hatch frames the setter's limited scope as a feature — 'I'm scheduling, they're solving' — which elevates the appointment. Holland's version does the opposite: it signals low authority before the pitch lands, which subtly devalues the meeting she is about to sell.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland is pre-empting scope-creep questions by setting expectations early. Rejected — the self-deprecation comes before any technical question is raised, so it is not a defensive move. It is a habit.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play this clip alongside the schema escape hatch. Ask Holland: 'What's the difference between these two framings? Which one makes Nancy want to show up?' The goal is to replace 'I don't know too much' with 'that's exactly what the specialist is built to answer.'

quote: K. Well, yeah, I can definitely get you set up to learn more.
stewart_read: Holland's bill-swap pitch at 03:51–04:14 is the call's best execution moment. She names the mechanism ('bill swap'), states the goal ('completely eliminate that bill and replace it with a cheaper solar bill'), and adds a credibility anchor ('if we can't save you money, we're not gonna try and sell it to you'). This pitch directly causes Nancy's peak engagement at 06:32. Holland should know she did this right.
coaching_implication: Open the 1-on-1 here before going to the gaps. 'This pitch worked — Nancy paraphrased it back to you at 06:32. That's the signal. Now let's talk about what to do when you get that signal.' Positive reinforcement before correction keeps the rep coachable.

quote: we could try doing, like, a three way call with Dominion if you wanted to. Other than that, I'm not too sure how else you would be able to get that usage. Or if you wanted to call Dominion and get it, then you can just send it over to me.
stewart_read: Nancy has no online account and no paper bill — a clear trigger for the verbal-readback pivot per schema bill_collection.alternative_paths.elderly_low_tech_protocol. Holland instead offers a three-way call with Dominion (high friction, uncertain outcome) and a 'call them yourself' option (defers the problem entirely). Neither path is the schema-recommended verbal readback. The lead-type caveat applies here: if this is an organic lead, Holland should have booked the appointment and deferred bill collection to the specialist site visit, bypassing this wall entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland is exploring all paths before giving up. Rejected — the schema specifies a pivot within 2 minutes of friction, and Holland never reaches the verbal-readback option at all.
coaching_implication: Walk Holland through the three alternative paths in the schema. Ask: 'What would you do if the customer had a paper bill in front of them right now?' Then: 'What if this is an organic lead — do you need the bill at all to book?' The goal is to give her a decision tree, not just a script.

quote: So essentially, I'm swapping one bill for the other bill.
stewart_read: Nancy's paraphrase is the clearest booking signal in the call. She has internalized the value proposition, is asking follow-up questions ('Does the solar bill ever go down?'), and is leaning in. This is the moment to say 'That's exactly it — and the specialist will show you your specific numbers. What does your Monday look like?' Holland instead answers the follow-up question and continues explaining, letting the peak moment pass without a booking attempt.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland is building more conviction before asking for the appointment. Rejected — she never asks for the appointment at all, so there is no later move being set up.
coaching_implication: Use this clip to teach booking-signal recognition. Ask Holland: 'What did Nancy just tell you here? What should you have done in the next 10 seconds?' The answer is a direct calendar ask, not another explanation.

quote: I I really don't know. That is a really good question, and I can notate that down for my specialist as well. I don't wanna accidentally mislead you with any information.
stewart_read: Holland correctly deflects a closer-tier question ('does the solar bill get reviewed every year?') to the specialist and adds a credibility line ('I don't wanna accidentally mislead you'). This is the right strategy — but Holland stops one move short. The deflection is the setup; the booking ask is the payoff. 'That's exactly what the specialist will go over with you — let's get you on the calendar so you have those answers by Tuesday' converts the deflection into a sit. Holland lands the setup and skips the payoff.
coaching_implication: This is the clearest teachable moment for the 'deflect-then-book' sequence. Show Holland the two-step: (1) 'Great question, the specialist handles that' — she does this. (2) 'Let's get you on the calendar so you have the answer by [day]' — she skips this. The gap is one sentence.

quote: Alright. Yeah. That sounds good. I can just I'll just text you. That way, you have my contact information. And then if you can reach them on Monday, just feel free to send over that bill. Okay?
stewart_read: This is not a conditional booking — it is a non-booking with a personal-contact anti-pattern layered on top. Holland gives out her personal contact information for bill follow-up (the exact pattern flagged in schema bill_collection.stewart_automated_bill_capture.detection_today), sets no callback time, and establishes no appointment anchor. The call ends with Nancy having four unresolved program questions, no scheduled next touch, and Holland's personal number as the only thread connecting them.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland is respecting Nancy's pace and will follow up Monday with a booking ask once the bill arrives. Rejected — there is no locked time for that follow-up, no appointment structure, and no 'is that fair' handshake. This is dissolution, not deferral.
coaching_implication: Two things to address: (1) Personal number exposure — walk Holland to the schema flag and explain the privacy/scale risk. (2) The missing booking attempt — even without the bill, Holland could have said 'Let me go ahead and pencil you in for Tuesday at 10 — if the bill isn't in by then, we'll reschedule, but let's hold the time.' A locked callback with a conditional release is a sit; an open-ended 'send it over' is a ghost.
